title: Unlock the Right Questions
prompt: I need to [accomplish a goal] but I'm not sure where to start. Can you ask me a few questions to help me think through what I actually need?
tip: Don't overthink your answer — just respond naturally. The AI will keep asking until the picture is clear.

title: Break Down the Overwhelm
prompt: I'm feeling stuck on [problem or project]. I don't know where to begin. Help me break this into smaller steps I can actually act on today.
tip: Be honest about what's stopping you — naming the blocker out loud often makes it easier to move past it.

title: Figure Out What You Actually Need
prompt: I want to [general goal], but I'm not sure if I need a plan, a document, a decision, or just to think out loud. Can you help me figure out what kind of help I actually need right now?
tip: This is a great first message for any fuzzy situation — it helps you show up with more clarity.

title: Argue the Other Side
prompt: Here is my plan / position: [describe it]. I want you to argue the strongest possible case against it. What am I missing? What would a skeptic say?
tip: Read the pushback without defending yourself first — the goal is to find the real holes, not win the argument.

title: Find the Hidden Risks
prompt: I'm about to [make a decision or take an action]. What are the 3 most likely ways this could go wrong that I'm probably not thinking about?
tip: Be specific about what you're doing — vague inputs produce generic risks. The more detail, the more useful the answer.

title: Refine the Draft
prompt: Here's what I have so far: [paste draft]. What's the weakest part? What would make this clearer or more persuasive? Don't rewrite it — just tell me what to fix.
tip: Asking the AI not to rewrite keeps you in the driver's seat — you improve it yourself with better direction.

title: Multi-Step Decision Memo
prompt: I need to make a decision about [topic]. Step 1: Ask me 5 questions to understand the situation fully. Then wait for my answers before doing anything else.
tip: Resist the urge to answer all 5 questions in one message — take them one at a time for a richer conversation.

title: Build on the AI's Last Answer
prompt: Take the plan you just wrote and now write me a list of the 5 most important questions a skeptical stakeholder would ask. I'll use that list to pressure-test the plan before I present it.
tip: This works best right after the AI produces something — use it as a second pass, not a standalone prompt.

title: Structured Options Analysis
prompt: I'm weighing these options: [list them]. For each one, give me: the strongest argument for it, the strongest argument against it, and one condition under which it becomes the obvious right choice.
tip: Include a 'do nothing' option in your list — it's often the most honest choice to stress-test.

title: Prospect Research Brief
prompt:
You are a B2B sales researcher. Before I get on a discovery call, help me build a quick research brief on this prospect.

Company: [company name]
Industry: [industry]
Approximate size: [number of employees or revenue range]

Please provide:
1. What this type of company likely cares about most in relation to [your product or service]
2. Common pain points for this industry relevant to [your solution]
3. 3 smart questions I should ask to uncover their current situation
4. Any regulatory or compliance angles I should be aware of (e.g. HIPAA for healthcare)

Keep it concise — I need this before a call, not a research paper.
tip: Add any news you've seen about the company or industry for even sharper insights.

title: Discovery Call Prep Questions
prompt:
I'm preparing for a discovery call with a prospect in the [industry] industry. They're evaluating [your product or service category].

Generate 10 thoughtful discovery questions that will help me:
- Understand their current situation and its pain points
- Uncover their real business drivers (cost, reliability, compliance, growth, efficiency)
- Identify key decision-makers and the buying process
- Discover any urgency or triggering events

Format as a numbered list. Include a brief note on what each question is trying to uncover.
tip: Run this once per industry vertical and save the output — it becomes a reusable call prep template.

title: Competitive Positioning
prompt:
I'm selling [your product or service] and I'm going up against [competitor name] in a deal.

Please help me with competitive positioning:
1. What are [competitor name]'s typical strengths that they lead with?
2. What are their most commonly cited weaknesses or gaps?
3. Where does a high-touch, consultative provider have an advantage?
4. What 2-3 questions should I ask the prospect that will naturally surface where [competitor name] falls short?
5. How should I handle it if the prospect says "[competitor name] is cheaper"?

Be direct and practical. I'm in a competitive deal.
tip: Ask follow-up: 'What objections have you heard about [competitor] from other clients?' to get real-world talking points.

title: Proposal Executive Summary
prompt:
Write a compelling executive summary for a solution proposal.

Client name: [client name]
Industry: [industry]
Key pain points they shared: [list 2-3 pain points]
Number of users/seats: [number]
Solutions we're proposing: [list the products or services]

The executive summary should:
- Open with a statement that shows we understand their business problem
- Briefly describe the recommended solution and why it fits them specifically
- Call out 2-3 key outcomes they can expect
- Close with a forward-looking sentence about the partnership

Tone: confident, consultative, human. Not generic. Not full of jargon. 3-4 paragraphs max.
tip: Paste in exact quotes from your discovery call notes — The AI will weave them in to make it feel personal.

title: Follow-Up Email After Demo
prompt:
Write a follow-up email after a product or service demo.

Contact name: [contact name and title]
Key topics we covered in the demo: [list 2-3 things you showed or discussed]
Their main area of interest or question: [what they seemed most engaged by]
Proposed next step: [e.g. a follow-up call, a proposal, a reference call]

The email should:
- Be warm but professional — not salesy
- Reference something specific from the conversation so it doesn't feel templated
- Briefly recap what we discussed (2-3 bullet points)
- Make the next step crystal clear with a specific ask
- Be under 200 words

Subject line: also write 3 subject line options.
tip: Write this within 2 hours of the demo while details are fresh — then let your AI assistant polish it.

title: Onboarding Welcome Email
prompt:
Write a warm, professional onboarding welcome email for a new [Your Company] client.

Client name: [company name]
Industry: [industry]
Primary contact name: [first name]
Go-live date: [date]
Key solutions they're implementing: [list products or services]

The email should:
- Feel personal and genuinely excited (not like a form letter)
- Briefly tell them what to expect in the next few weeks
- Name their dedicated point of contact
- Give them 1 clear action item to take right now
- Close with confidence that this was a great decision

Tone: warm, organized, reassuring. Under 250 words.
tip: Personalize with one detail specific to their industry — it signals you understand their world.

title: QBR Talking Points
prompt:
Help me prepare talking points for a Quarterly Business Review with an existing client.

Client name: [client name]
Contract / account size: [approximate value or number of users]
Contract renewal timeline: [when does it come up?]
Known wins this quarter: [list any positive outcomes, resolved issues, features they're using well]
Known issues or concerns: [list anything that's been rocky or unresolved]

Please generate:
1. A strong opening that acknowledges the relationship and sets a positive tone
2. 3-4 talking points that reinforce the value we're delivering
3. A clear and honest way to address any known issues (without being defensive)
4. 2 questions to ask that will help us understand their evolving needs
5. A smooth transition to discussing the renewal

Keep it conversational — this is a meeting, not a report.
tip: Run this the day before the QBR so you can refine the talking points based on last-minute context.

title: At-Risk Client Strategy
prompt:
I need help developing a retention strategy for a client who is showing churn signals.

Client name: [client name]
What signals I'm seeing: [e.g. slower responses, complaints about billing, asking about competitors, low platform adoption, escalations]
Contract status: [months until renewal / currently up for renewal]
Relationship history: [brief note — have we had a good relationship? Any history of issues?]
Key stakeholders at the client: [names/roles if known]

Please help me:
1. Diagnose what's most likely driving the dissatisfaction based on the signals I've described
2. Suggest 3 specific actions I can take in the next 2 weeks to stabilize the relationship
3. Draft talking points for a "save the relationship" call
4. Identify what I should offer vs. what I should hold back
5. Flag any red flags that suggest the client may already be past the point of saving

Be direct. I need a real strategy, not platitudes.
tip: Share as much context as possible — the more specific the signals, the more targeted the strategy.

title: Product Update Announcement
prompt:
I need to communicate a product update to our clients in plain, accessible language.

[Paste the technical release notes or product update details here]

Please rewrite this for a non-technical business audience. The goal is to:
- Lead with what changed and why it matters to them (not how it was built)
- Use plain language — no technical jargon
- Format as a short email or announcement (under 200 words)
- Include a brief "what you need to do" section if any action is required
- End with who to contact with questions

Tone: informative, helpful, low-anxiety.
tip: Add 'Our clients are mostly [industry] businesses with [tech savvy level]' for a more tailored rewrite.

title: Escalation Summary for Leadership
prompt:
I need to write a concise escalation summary to share with leadership about an active client issue.

[Paste the relevant ticket notes, email thread, or describe the situation here]

Please produce a structured summary that includes:
1. **Situation** — What is happening and with which client (1-2 sentences)
2. **Impact** — What is the business/operational impact on the client
3. **Timeline** — Key events and when they occurred
4. **Current status** — Where things stand right now
5. **Actions taken** — What has been done so far
6. **Next steps** — What happens next and who owns it
7. **Ask of leadership** — What, if anything, do you need from them

Keep it factual, not defensive. Leadership needs clarity, not justification.
tip: Write this before you go into the leadership meeting, not during — it will help you stay calm and focused.

title: Partner Onboarding Checklist
prompt:
Create a comprehensive step-by-step onboarding checklist for a new [Your Company] channel partner.

Partner company name: [partner name]
Their industry focus / typical clients: [e.g. healthcare, SMB, enterprise, municipalities]
Partner type: [e.g. VAR, agent, MSP, consultant]

The checklist should cover:
1. Initial paperwork and agreements
2. Portal access and tools setup
3. Product training (what they need to know first)
4. First deal support (how we help them close their first client)
5. Ongoing resources and who their go-to contacts are

Format as a numbered checklist with clear ownership labels (Partner / Us). Keep it practical — something a new partner rep can actually follow.
tip: Customize one section at a time based on what the partner already knows — don't overwhelm them on day one.

title: Partner-Facing FAQ Document
prompt:
Generate a FAQ document that our channel partners can use when talking to their end clients about [Your Company].

Product or feature: [product name or service — e.g. your core platform, a specific feature, or a program]

The FAQ should:
- Answer the 8-10 most common questions an end client would ask
- Be written in plain language (not telecom jargon)
- Address concerns about switching, reliability, pricing model, and support
- Include a brief "Why [Your Company]" answer that partners can use verbatim
- Be formatted so partners can easily paste it into their own materials

Audience: business decision-makers who are non-technical.
tip: After generating, share it with one of your most active partners for a gut-check before distributing.

title: Deal Registration Status Email
prompt:
Draft a professional email to a channel partner about their deal registration status.

Partner name: [partner name / partner rep name]
End client name: [client name]
Deal registration status: [e.g. approved / pending review / needs more information / there's a conflict]
Any context or special instructions: [notes]

The email should:
- Be direct and friendly — these are our partners, treat them like colleagues
- Clearly communicate the status and what it means for them
- Include any next steps required (from them or from us)
- Set a realistic expectation for timeline if applicable

Under 150 words.
tip: Even a brief positive email when a deal is approved goes a long way in building partner loyalty.

title: 60-Minute Partner Training Agenda
prompt:
Build a 60-minute training agenda for a channel partner session on a product or feature.

Product / topic: [product name or feature — e.g. your platform, a key module, a new program]
Assumed knowledge level of the partners attending: [e.g. new to telecom / experienced VARs / mixed]
Goal of the session: [e.g. enable them to sell, enable them to demo, answer common client objections]

Please create a timed agenda (e.g. 0:00–0:10, etc.) that includes:
- An opening that gets them engaged quickly
- Product overview focused on business value (not just features)
- A competitive differentiator segment
- Objection handling practice
- Time for Q&A
- Clear takeaways and next steps

Include a note on what materials we should prepare in advance for each segment.
tip: Send the agenda to partners 48 hours in advance so they come with their real questions ready.

title: Partner Escalation Response
prompt:
Draft a professional response to a channel partner who is frustrated about a support delay or unresolved issue affecting their end client.

Issue description: [describe the problem]
Current status of the issue: [where things stand]
Expected resolution timeline: [your best honest estimate]
What has been done so far: [actions taken]
Relationship context: [is this a new partner, long-term partner, high-volume partner?]

The response should:
- Acknowledge their frustration genuinely — don't minimize it
- Take ownership without over-apologizing or making excuses
- Clearly state what is happening and what comes next
- Give them something specific they can tell their end client
- Close in a way that reinforces the partnership

Tone: accountable, calm, and partner-first. Not corporate-defensive.
tip: Read this out loud before sending — if it sounds like a PR statement, it needs more humanity.

title: Cutover Day Runbook
prompt:
Generate a structured day-of cutover checklist for a software or platform go-live.

Client name: [client name]
Number of locations: [number]
Scheduled go-live time: [time and timezone]
Key contacts day-of: [client contact name/number, internal PM name, technical lead]
Special considerations: [e.g. call center, 24/7 operation, specific departments going live first]

Please produce a time-sequenced runbook that includes:
- T-minus prep steps (night before / morning of)
- The cutover sequence itself (step by step)
- Verification checks after cutover
- Rollback trigger criteria (when do we escalate or pull back?)
- Communication checkpoints (when do we update the client?)
- Post-cutover monitoring window

Format as a checklist with time stamps and clear ownership labels (Client / PM / Technical).
tip: Walk through this with your technical team the day before so everyone knows their role before go-live.

title: Project Kickoff Agenda
prompt:
Create a kickoff meeting agenda for a new software implementation project.

Client name: [client name]
Number of users: [number]
Number of locations: [number]
Key stakeholders attending: [list names/roles if known]
Estimated project duration: [weeks/months]
Key implementation milestones: [list if known, e.g. discovery, provisioning, training, cutover]

The agenda should cover:
1. Introductions and roles
2. Project scope and objectives review
3. Timeline and milestone walkthrough
4. Roles and responsibilities (client vs. [Your Company])
5. Communication cadence (how we'll stay in sync)
6. Open questions and parking lot

Include suggested time allocations for each section. Total meeting time: 60 minutes.
tip: Send this to the client 3 days before the meeting and ask them to add agenda items — it sets a collaborative tone.

title: Weekly Status Update to Client
prompt:
Write a professional weekly project status update email for an implementation client.

Project status: [On Track / At Risk / Delayed]
Client name: [client name]
Key milestones completed this week: [list]
Key milestones planned for next week: [list]
Current blockers or risks: [list, or write "none"]
Any decisions needed from the client: [list, or write "none"]

The email should:
- Open with a clear status signal (on track, at risk, etc.) — don't bury the lead
- Be scannable — use short bullets, not paragraphs
- Be honest about risks without being alarmist
- Close with a specific ask or next action if one exists

Under 200 words. Subject line: also suggest one.
tip: Send this on the same day every week — consistency builds trust with clients more than any individual update.

title: Risk Log Summary for Leadership
prompt:
Summarize open project risks for a leadership review in a clear, scannable format.

[Paste your current risk log, or list the risks in bullet form here]

For each risk, please produce:
- **Risk**: one sentence description
- **Likelihood**: Low / Medium / High
- **Impact if it occurs**: one sentence
- **Current mitigation**: what's being done
- **Owner**: who is responsible for managing it
- **Status**: Open / Being Monitored / Escalated

Then add a brief executive summary (2-3 sentences) that tells leadership what they most need to pay attention to right now.
tip: Sort risks by impact before presenting — leadership wants to know the top 2-3, not a list of 15.

title: Post-Implementation Lessons Learned
prompt:
Help me write a post-implementation lessons learned document for a completed project.

What went well: [list your observations]
What didn't go as planned: [list challenges, delays, or failures]
What you'd do differently next time: [your reflections]
Any client feedback received: [positive or critical]
Project type and rough timeline: [brief description]

Please structure this as:
1. **Project Summary** (2 sentences)
2. **What Worked Well** (with brief explanations of why)
3. **What We'd Improve** (with specific, actionable recommendations — not vague)
4. **Recommendations for Future Projects of This Type**
5. **Client Relationship Notes** (what did we learn about managing this type of client?)

Make this genuinely useful, not a checkbox exercise. Be candid.
tip: Do this within 2 weeks of project close, while memories are fresh — waiting longer means losing the most valuable details.

title: SEO Blog Post Outline
prompt:
Create an SEO-optimized blog post outline for [Your Company].

Topic: [topic — e.g. a trend, a challenge, or a how-to relevant to your industry]
Target audience: [e.g. IT directors, operations managers, facility administrators]
Primary keyword to target: [keyword if known, or ask your AI assistant to suggest one]
Approximate word count: [e.g. 800-1200 words]

The outline should include:
- A compelling H1 title (give 3 options)
- Meta description (under 160 characters)
- H2 and H3 subheadings that reflect how the audience searches
- Key points to cover in each section
- A suggested CTA at the end

Note: [Your Company] is a [brief description of your brand/positioning]. Tone should be authoritative but accessible — not overly technical.
tip: Add 'Here are 3 questions we get asked about this topic:' before sending — it makes the outline much more targeted.

title: LinkedIn Post
prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post for [Your Company] about a company update, win, or announcement.

What's the news: [describe the update or announcement in plain language — a new client, a product launch, an award, a team hire, an insight]
Tone preference: [e.g. celebratory / educational / thought leadership / behind-the-scenes]
Call to action: [what do you want people to do — comment, learn more, reach out?]

Please write:
1. A primary version (150-200 words, professional but warm)
2. A shorter punchy version (under 100 words)
3. 5 hashtag suggestions

The post should feel like it comes from a real person at a company that genuinely cares about their clients — not a press release.
tip: The shorter version often performs better — test both and see which one gets more engagement.

title: Customer Case Study Draft
prompt:
Write a customer case study for [Your Company] based on these details:

Client industry: [industry — keep anonymous or use name if approved]
The challenge they faced: [describe their communications problem or pain point before working with us]
Solution deployed: [what we implemented — describe the product, service, or program]
Outcome / results: [what changed for them — quantify if possible: e.g. reduced dropped calls by X%, streamlined to 1 platform]
Any direct quotes or approval status: [do we have a quote, or should you generate a plausible placeholder?]

Please structure as:
1. **Headline** (3 options)
2. **The Challenge** (1-2 paragraphs)
3. **The Solution** (1-2 paragraphs)
4. **The Results** (bullet points + a brief narrative)
5. **Client Quote** (real or [PLACEHOLDER])
6. **Why [Your Company]** (2-3 sentences)

Length: ~500 words. Tone: confident, client-centric, not salesy.
tip: Get one real data point from the client (any metric) — a single number makes the whole case study more credible.

title: Email Campaign Subject Lines
prompt:
Generate 10 email subject line variations for a marketing campaign.

Campaign topic: [what is the email about?]
Target audience: [who is receiving this — industry, role, or stage in the funnel]
Goal of the email: [open a conversation / drive a demo request / announce something / re-engage cold leads]
Tone range: [professional only / can be playful / urgency-driven / curiosity-driven]

For each subject line, note the tactic being used (e.g. curiosity gap, social proof, direct value, urgency, question).

Also suggest:
- 1 preview text to pair with the top subject line
- Which subject line you'd A/B test first and why
tip: Ask Claude to rewrite your top pick three different ways — sometimes the second variation is the winner.

title: Industry Landing Page Copy
prompt:
Write web copy for an industry-specific landing page for [Your Company].

Industry: [industry name — e.g. healthcare, education, legal, logistics, finance, retail]
Their top communication pain points: [list 2-3 things this industry struggles with around phone/communications]
Key solutions we offer this industry: [list the products, services, or programs you offer this vertical]
Page goal: [drive a demo request / contact form / phone call]

Please write:
1. **Hero Section**: H1 headline + subheadline + CTA button text
2. **Pain Point Section**: 3 pain points with brief copy for each
3. **Solution Section**: How [Your Company] addresses these (3 benefit statements)
4. **Social Proof Hook**: A placeholder for a client quote or stat
5. **CTA Section**: Closing copy + CTA

Tone: direct, knowledgeable, human. [Your Company] is a trusted partner, not just a vendor.
tip: Start with your best-performing industry and refine the copy with real client language before building the page.

title: Job Description
prompt:
Write a job description for an open role at [Your Company].

Role title: [job title]
Key responsibilities: [list 4-5 main things this person will do]
Must-have experience: [list 2-3 non-negotiables]
Nice-to-have skills: [list 1-3 preferred qualifications]
Team / reports to: [if known]
Work arrangement: [remote / hybrid / on-site — include location if applicable]

The job description should:
- Open with a compelling "about the role" paragraph (not a generic company boilerplate)
- Use clear, human language — not bureaucratic HR-speak
- Be specific about what success looks like in the role
- Attract people who want to grow, not just fill a seat
- Be appropriately concise — under 400 words

Also suggest 3 job posting titles to use for maximum search visibility.
tip: Share the draft with the hiring manager before posting — they'll catch missing details that save you time later.

title: Behavioral Interview Questions
prompt:
Generate a behavioral interview question set for a specific role.

Role title: [role title, e.g. Customer Success Manager / Sales Representative / Implementation PM]
Top 3 skills or qualities you're hiring for: [list them — e.g. accountability, communication under pressure, technical aptitude]
Any specific scenarios this role will face: [e.g. handling difficult clients, managing competing priorities, working cross-functionally]

Please generate:
- 3 behavioral questions per skill area (using the STAR format: Situation, Task, Action, Result)
- 1 follow-up probe question for each main question
- What a strong answer looks like for each question (brief notes for the interviewer)

Format as a structured interview guide.
tip: Use these as a guide, not a script — the best interviews feel like conversations, not interrogations.

title: New Hire Announcement Email
prompt:
Draft a warm internal email announcing a new team member joining [Your Company].

New hire's name: [name]
Role / title: [role]
Start date: [date]
Team or department: [team they're joining]
One fun fact about them: [something personal they're comfortable sharing]
Manager's name: [optional]

The email should:
- Feel like it was written by a real person who is genuinely excited, not a form letter
- Give the team enough context to know who this person is and how to connect with them
- Invite people to reach out and welcome them
- Be under 150 words

Subject line: also suggest one.
tip: Ask the new hire to share their own fun fact before you write this — it makes the intro far more memorable.

title: Performance Review Talking Points
prompt:
Help me prepare for a performance review conversation with an employee.

Employee's role: [title and brief description of what they do]
Their key areas of strength this review period: [list 2-3 specific things they've done well]
Areas to develop or improve: [list 1-2 honest development areas — be specific, not vague]
Any significant events this period: [promotions, misses, changes in scope, difficult situations]
Tone goal: [e.g. primarily developmental / recognition-focused / course-correction needed]

Please generate:
1. An opening that sets a positive, candid tone
2. Talking points for each strength (with language that feels specific, not generic)
3. Talking points for development areas (honest but constructive — not a pile-on)
4. 2 forward-looking questions to invite their input
5. A closing that leaves them feeling clear on expectations and supported

Note: This is a conversation guide, not a script.
tip: Practice the development area talking points out loud first — they're the ones most people stumble over.

title: Policy Plain-Language Summary
prompt:
Rewrite a company policy document in plain, accessible language that employees will actually read.

[Paste the policy text here]

Please rewrite it so that:
- A new employee with no HR background can understand it in one read
- The key rules and expectations are crystal clear
- Any actions required of employees are stated as simple steps
- The tone is respectful and professional — not threatening or bureaucratic
- It is formatted with clear headers and bullets where appropriate

Also add a "Key Takeaways" box at the top with 3-5 bullet points summarizing the most important points.

Keep the length similar to the original, or shorter.
tip: Test it: send to one non-HR employee and ask 'What are you supposed to do based on this?' — their answer reveals gaps.

title: Monthly Report Narrative
prompt:
Write a plain-language narrative to accompany a financial summary report.

Please describe or paste the key numbers:
[e.g. Revenue: $X vs. plan of $Y (Z% variance), Key cost drivers, notable changes month-over-month, any one-time items]

The narrative should:
- Lead with the headline: are we above or below plan, and by how much?
- Explain the key drivers in plain language (not accounting speak)
- Note any one-time or unusual items that affected the numbers
- Be forward-looking: what does this mean for next month or quarter?
- Be under 300 words

Audience: leadership team, some of whom are not finance professionals.
tip: Write a one-sentence 'so what' at the top before you run this — it helps the AI stay focused on what matters most.

title: Vendor Comparison
prompt:
Help me compare two vendors or pricing options so I can make a clear recommendation.

Vendor A: [name and key details — pricing, terms, capabilities, support model]
Vendor B: [name and key details — pricing, terms, capabilities, support model]
Decision criteria (what matters most): [e.g. total cost, contract flexibility, support quality, integration capability]
Our context: [brief note on what we're using this for and any constraints]

Please produce:
1. A side-by-side comparison table
2. A brief analysis of where each vendor wins and loses
3. A recommended choice with reasoning
4. Any risks or caveats in the recommendation
5. Questions I should ask each vendor before finalizing

Be direct — I need a recommendation, not just a summary.
tip: Add 'Our biggest risk with this decision is X' to get the AI to weight that factor more heavily in the analysis.

title: Contract Plain-Language Summary
prompt:
Summarize a vendor or client contract in plain language so I can understand what I'm agreeing to.

[Paste the contract clauses, key sections, or full contract text here]

Please produce:
1. **What this contract covers** — scope and services in plain terms
2. **Key obligations** — what each party must do
3. **Payment terms** — how much, when, and under what conditions
4. **Termination clauses** — how and when either party can exit
5. **Liability and indemnification** — what are we on the hook for?
6. **Anything unusual or worth flagging** — clauses that stand out as one-sided, vague, or risky
7. **My top 3 questions for legal review** before signing

Note: This is not legal advice — it's a plain-language summary to help me have an informed conversation with legal counsel.
tip: Always have legal review any contract before signing — use this summary to ask better questions, not replace that review.

title: Budget Justification
prompt:
Write a business case to justify a budget request.

What I'm requesting: [describe the spend — tool, headcount, service, equipment, etc.]
Total cost: [amount and whether it's one-time or recurring]
Business reason / problem this solves: [why do we need this?]
Expected ROI or benefit: [time saved, revenue enabled, risk reduced, cost avoided — be as specific as possible]
Alternatives considered: [did you look at other options? why is this the right one?]
Urgency: [is there a deadline or consequence to not acting?]

Please write a 1-page business case that:
- Leads with the ask and the business justification
- Quantifies the benefit where possible
- Addresses likely objections
- Closes with a clear recommendation and next step

Tone: confident and factual.
tip: The strongest budget cases quantify the cost of inaction — ask your AI assistant to add that angle if you forgot to include it.

title: Standard Operating Procedure
prompt:
Help me document a recurring operational process as a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP).

Process name: [what is this process called?]
Who performs this process: [role or team]
How often it runs: [daily / weekly / monthly / as-needed]
Rough steps as I know them: [describe the process in rough notes — don't worry about perfect formatting]

Please produce an SOP that includes:
1. **Purpose** — why this process exists
2. **Scope** — who does it and when
3. **Prerequisites** — what needs to be in place before starting
4. **Step-by-Step Instructions** — numbered, clear, actionable
5. **Quality Checks** — how do you know it was done correctly?
6. **Common Issues and Fixes** — 2-3 things that often go wrong
7. **Owner** — who is responsible for keeping this SOP current

Format for easy scanning.
tip: Have the person who actually does the process review the draft — they'll catch the steps you described incorrectly.

title: All-Hands Talking Points
prompt:
Generate talking points for a company all-hands meeting.

Theme or focus of the meeting: [e.g. Q1 results, new strategy, culture reset, celebrating a milestone]
Key updates to communicate: [list 3-4 things leadership needs to share]
Tone: [e.g. energizing / candid / forward-looking / recognition-focused]
Anything sensitive to navigate carefully: [layoffs, a difficult quarter, a major change — if applicable]
Audience: [all staff across Sales, CS, Operations, Marketing, Finance, HR — approximately X people]

Please produce:
1. An opening that gets the room's attention and sets the right tone
2. Talking point for each key update (2-3 sentences each, designed to be spoken, not read)
3. A section that acknowledges what's been hard (if applicable) — honest, not dismissive
4. A forward-looking close that leaves people feeling clear and motivated
5. 2-3 moments to invite participation or questions

Total speaking time: approximately 20 minutes.
tip: Practice the opening out loud — the first 60 seconds of an all-hands sets the entire emotional tone.

title: Strategic Decision Framework
prompt:
Help me structure a decision memo for an important business decision.

The decision: [describe the decision that needs to be made]
Options under consideration: [list 2-4 options, including the "do nothing" option if relevant]
Key trade-offs: [what are the main tensions — cost vs. speed, risk vs. growth, short-term vs. long-term?]
Stakeholders who will be affected: [who has a stake in this decision?]
Timeline: [when does this decision need to be made?]
What we know: [key facts, data, or constraints]
What we don't know: [uncertainties that affect this decision]

Please produce a structured decision memo that:
1. Frames the decision clearly (what exactly are we deciding?)
2. Lays out each option with pros, cons, and risks
3. Identifies the key assumptions behind each option
4. Makes a recommended choice with clear reasoning
5. Notes what would need to be true for a different option to be better
6. Proposes next steps if the recommendation is approved
tip: Share this with one trusted colleague before presenting — a second perspective often reveals a hidden option.

title: Board Update Summary
prompt:
Draft a concise board-level update for [Your Company].

Period covered: [quarter / month]
Performance highlights: [key wins, revenue milestones, notable client additions or renewals]
Challenges: [what headwinds are we facing — be honest]
Key initiatives in progress: [what strategic work is underway]
Asks of the board: [anything you need from them — decisions, introductions, guidance]
Metrics to include: [list any specific numbers you want highlighted]

Please produce a structured board update (approximately 1 page) that:
- Leads with the most important headline
- Is scannable (use headers and bullets)
- Is honest about challenges without dwelling on them
- Frames asks clearly and specifically
- Ends with a clear outlook statement

Tone: confident, transparent, concise. Boards value clarity over polish.
tip: Board members read many updates — lead with the one thing you most want them to remember.

title: Competitive Landscape Snapshot
prompt:
Summarize the current competitive landscape in [your industry or market].

Focus area: [choose one or more: pricing dynamics / product features / market positioning / a specific competitor / emerging threats]
Our context: [[Your Company] is a [brief description — e.g. regional/national/B2B provider focused on X, serving Y industries or customer types]]

Please provide:
1. A brief market overview (where the space is heading)
2. Key competitors and how they position themselves
3. Where high-touch regional providers have a natural advantage
4. Where we face the most pressure
5. 2-3 strategic implications for how we should think about our positioning

Note: Use your knowledge of the industry up to your training cutoff. Flag anything I should verify with current sources.
tip: Follow up with: 'What is the single most important thing a company in this space should do to stay competitive?' for a sharper strategic lens.

title: Culture Pulse Internal Message
prompt:
Write an internal message from leadership to the [Your Company] team on culture, values, or a milestone.

The occasion or theme: [e.g. company anniversary, recognizing a team effort, reinforcing a value, entering a new year/quarter, acknowledging a hard stretch]
Key message you want to land: [what do you most want people to feel or understand after reading this?]
Any specific team or individual to recognize: [optional]
Tone: [choose: inspiring / warm / candid / celebratory / grounding]

The message should:
- Sound like a real human being wrote it — not corporate-speak
- Be under 250 words
- Name something specific (an example, a moment, a behavior) that makes it feel real
- End with a call to connection — invite people to respond, share, or reach out

Also suggest a subject line.
tip: The most memorable culture messages name a specific person or moment — generalities don't land, specifics do.

title: Morning Email Triage
prompt:
I've connected my email via the Email Connector. Please review my unread emails and help me triage them.

For each unread email, please:
1. Summarize what it's about in one sentence
2. Flag the urgency: Urgent / Needs a response today / Can wait / FYI only
3. Identify if any action is required, and what that action is
4. Group them by: Needs my response / Needs someone else's attention / No action needed

Start with the most urgent. I want to process my inbox in under 10 minutes.
tip: Run this at the start of your day before opening individual emails — it prevents inbox rabbit holes.

title: Draft Reply from Email Thread
prompt:
I've connected my email via the Email Connector. Please read the email thread I'm going to share and draft a reply for me.

[Paste the email thread here, or describe which thread to access]

My intent with the reply: [e.g. agree to the meeting / push back on the timeline / ask for clarification / close the loop / decline politely]
Tone: [professional / warm / direct / formal]
Any specific points I want to make sure are included: [list them, or write "none"]

Please draft a reply that:
- Responds to the key points raised
- Is clear and appropriately concise
- Sounds like me — not like a form letter
- Includes a clear next step if one is needed

Also suggest a subject line update if the thread has drifted off topic.
tip: Add 'My usual communication style is X' once to give your AI assistant a consistent voice to match going forward.

title: Action Items from Email
prompt:
I've connected my email via the Email Connector. Please scan my recent emails (last [X days / X emails]) and pull out all action items.

For each action item found, please list:
- What needs to be done
- Who it's assigned to (me, or someone else I need to follow up with)
- Any deadline mentioned (or "no deadline stated")
- The email or thread it came from

Then give me a prioritized to-do list based on urgency and what's waiting on me specifically.

Ignore newsletters, automated notifications, and anything clearly informational.
tip: Run this on Friday afternoon to build your task list for the following week — it takes 2 minutes and nothing slips through.

title: Summarize a Document or File
prompt:
I have a document in my connected files that I need to understand quickly.

[Paste the document content here, or describe the file you want summarized]

Please provide:
1. A 3-5 sentence executive summary — what is this document about and what does it conclude or recommend?
2. Key facts, numbers, or decisions it contains (bullet points)
3. Any action items or deadlines mentioned
4. Anything I should pay particular attention to or flag for review

If the document is a contract or agreement, also note: the key parties, the term length, and any clauses that stand out.
tip: Use this before any meeting where you were sent a document to review — it takes 30 seconds and helps you show up prepared.

title: Compare Two Documents
prompt:
I have two versions of a document in my connected files. Please compare them and tell me what changed.

[Paste Document Version 1 here — or describe it]

[Paste Document Version 2 here — or describe it]

Please tell me:
1. What was added in the new version
2. What was removed or changed
3. Whether the changes are substantive (affect meaning, obligations, or decisions) or cosmetic (formatting, typos)
4. Any changes I should specifically review before approving or signing
5. Your overall read: is Version 2 better, worse, or roughly equivalent to Version 1?
tip: Especially useful for contract redlines — paste both versions and ask the AI to flag any new liability language.

title: Meeting Prep from Recent Emails
prompt:
I have a meeting coming up with [person/team/client name] on [topic]. I've connected my email via the Email Connector. Please review recent email threads involving [their name / this topic] to help me prepare.

Based on our email history, please tell me:
1. What have we been discussing or working on with this person/team?
2. Are there any open issues, unresolved questions, or commitments I made that I should follow up on?
3. What's the most recent status of anything we're working on together?
4. Are there any tensions, pending decisions, or things I should be careful about going into this meeting?

Then: suggest 3 specific things I should bring up or have ready for this meeting.
tip: Run this 30 minutes before any client call — it takes less time than re-reading a thread and gives you better recall.
